## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]  
**Define Controllable Canonical Form (CCF). Write the general structure of the system matrices $(A_c, B_c, C_c)$ for a third-order system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Controllable Canonical Form is a state-space representation in which the input matrix $B_c$ takes a standard unit-vector form, making the controllability property structurally evident.
>
> For a 3rd-order system with characteristic polynomial $s^3 + a_1 s^2 + a_2 s + a_3$:
>
> $$A_c = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 & -a_3 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_c = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & c_2 & c_3 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> where $c_i = b_i - a_i b_0$ are derived from the numerator coefficients.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]  
**State the duality relationship between Controllable Canonical Form and Observable Canonical Form. Mention two structural differences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Duality Theorem:** If $(A_c, B_c, C_c)$ is a system in CCF, then $(A_c^T, C_c^T, B_c^T)$ is automatically in OCF.
>
> **Structural Differences:**
> 1. In CCF, the 1's in $A$ lie on the **sub-diagonal**; in OCF, they lie on the **super-diagonal**.
> 2. In CCF, $B_c = [1, 0, \dots, 0]^T$ and $C_c$ contains the numerator coefficients; in OCF, the roles are reversed — $C_o = [1, 0, \dots, 0]$ and $B_o$ contains the numerator coefficients.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**(a)** For the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{2s^2 + 8s + 6}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$, obtain the **Controllable Canonical Form** state-space representation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same transfer function, obtain the **Observable Canonical Form** and **Jordan Canonical Form**. Comment on the stability of the system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Identify the coefficients** [1 mark]

Comparing with the standard form $G(s) = \dfrac{b_0 s^3 + b_1 s^2 + b_2 s + b_3}{s^3 + a_1 s^2 + a_2 s + a_3}$:

$$a_1 = 6, \quad a_2 = 11, \quad a_3 = 6, \quad b_0 = 0, \quad b_1 = 2, \quad b_2 = 8, \quad b_3 = 6$$

**Step 2: Write the $A_c$ matrix** [2 marks]

$$A_c = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 & -a_3 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -6 & -11 & -6 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Write the $B_c$ matrix** [1 mark]

$$B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute $C_c$ using $c_i = b_i - a_i b_0$** [2 marks]

Since $b_0 = 0$, the formula simplifies to $c_i = b_i$:

$$C_c = \begin{bmatrix} b_3 & b_2 & b_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 8 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Write $D_c$** [1 mark]

$$D_c = b_0 = 0$$

**Final CCF Representation:**

$$\dot{X} = \begin{bmatrix} -6 & -11 & -6 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} X + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} u, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 8 & 2 \end{bmatrix} X$$

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Build the OCF by duality** [2 marks]

Apply the duality transformation $A_o = A_c^T$, $B_o = C_c^T$, $C_o = B_c^T$:

$$A_o = \begin{bmatrix} -6 & 1 & 0 \\ -11 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_o = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 8 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_o = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_o = 0$$

**Step 2: Find the eigenvalues for the JCF** [2 marks]

The characteristic polynomial is $s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6 = 0$. Factoring:

$$(s+1)(s+2)(s+3) = 0$$

Therefore, the eigenvalues are:
$$\lambda_1 = -1, \quad \lambda_2 = -2, \quad \lambda_3 = -3$$

All three are **real and distinct**, so the JCF is purely diagonal.

**Step 3: Construct the JCF** [2 marks]

$$A_J = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

For the input matrix, apply the modal transformation $B_J = M^{-1} B_c$, where $M$ is the matrix of eigenvectors. Since the exam typically accepts the standard form:

$$B_J = \begin{bmatrix} \beta_1 \\ \beta_2 \\ \beta_3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_J = \begin{bmatrix} \gamma_1 & \gamma_2 & \gamma_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

The specific values of $\beta_i, \gamma_i$ depend on the eigenvectors, but a sufficient answer is the diagonal $A_J$ and acknowledgment that all three eigenvalues are real and distinct.

**Step 4: Comment on stability** [1 mark]

Since all eigenvalues satisfy $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ (i.e., $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$ are all negative real numbers), the system is **asymptotically stable**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** A system has the Controllable Canonical Form: $A_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$, $B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $C_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $D_c = 0$. Obtain the **transfer function** of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system $G(s) = \dfrac{s+2}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$, obtain the **Controllable Canonical Form** and the **Jordan Canonical Form**. Show that the JCF is diagonal in this case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Recall the TF formula from state-space** [1 mark]

$$G(s) = C_c (sI - A_c)^{-1} B_c + D_c$$

**Step 2: Compute $sI - A_c$** [1 mark]

$$sI - A_c = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & s & -1 \\ 6 & 11 & s+6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute the inverse $(sI - A_c)^{-1}$** [2 marks]

Using the adjoint method or cofactor expansion, one obtains:

$$(sI - A_c)^{-1} = \frac{1}{\Delta(s)} \begin{bmatrix} s^2+6s+11 & s+6 & 1 \\ -6 & s(s+6) & s \\ 6s & -11s-6 & s^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\Delta(s) = s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6$.

**Step 4: Multiply and extract the transfer function** [2 marks]

$$G(s) = C_c (sI - A_c)^{-1} B_c = \frac{1}{\Delta(s)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cdot \\ \cdot \\ \cdot \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$$

**Step 5: Final answer** [1 mark]

$$\boxed{G(s) = \frac{1}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}}$$

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Identify coefficients** [1 mark]

For $G(s) = \dfrac{s+2}{s^2+3s+2}$, we have $n=2$, $a_1 = 3$, $a_2 = 2$, $b_0 = 0$, $b_1 = 0$, $b_2 = 1$.

**Step 2: Construct the CCF** [2 marks]

$$A_c = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -2 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_c = \begin{bmatrix} b_2 & b_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_c = 0$$

**Step 3: Find the eigenvalues** [2 marks]

The characteristic polynomial is $s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2) = 0$, giving $\lambda_1 = -1$, $\lambda_2 = -2$.

**Step 4: Construct the JCF** [2 marks]

Since the eigenvalues are **distinct**, the JCF is purely diagonal:

$$A_J = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_J = M^{-1} B_c, \quad C_J = C_c M$$

The JCF is shown to be diagonal because the geometric multiplicity equals the algebraic multiplicity for each eigenvalue (a consequence of distinct eigenvalues).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Canonical Form Questions:**
> 1. **Sign errors in $A_c$:** The top row of $A_c$ contains **$-a_1, -a_2, \dots$** (negative of denominator coefficients). Writing $+a_1$ is a guaranteed **−2 mark** deduction.
> 2. **Confusing the order of $C_c$:** Students often write $C_c = [b_1, b_2, b_3]$ when it should be $[b_n, b_{n-1}, \dots, b_1]$ (reversed order, paired with $[-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \dots, -a_1]$). This costs **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting $D_c = b_0$:** The direct feedthrough term is easily missed. Examiners allocate **1 mark** explicitly for $D$.
> 4. **OCF without duality verification:** When asked to derive OCF from CCF, simply writing $A_o = A_c^T$ is enough — but students often try to re-derive from the TF, making sign/ordering errors. Use the **duality theorem** (saves time and marks).
> 5. **Jordan form for complex eigenvalues:** KTU problems often use real-coefficient systems, so the JCF should be in **real block form**, not complex diagonal form. Always convert complex conjugate pairs $\sigma \pm j\omega$ into $2 \times 2$ real Jordan blocks $\begin{bmatrix} \sigma & \omega \\ -\omega & \sigma \end{bmatrix}$.
> 6. **Skipping stability comment:** Even if not explicitly asked, ending a JCF problem with "All eigenvalues have negative real parts, so the system is stable" earns a **bonus half-mark** from lenient examiners.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CCF** is the easiest canonical form to construct — just read coefficients directly from the transfer function.
- **OCF** is the **transpose-dual** of CCF: swap $A \leftrightarrow A^T$, $B \leftrightarrow C^T$, $C \leftrightarrow B^T$.
- **JCF** exposes the **modes** of the system (eigenvalues); useful for modal analysis and stability testing.
- The **characteristic polynomial** is invariant under similarity transformation: $\det(sI - A_c) = \det(sI - A_o) = \det(sI - A_J) = s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \dots + a_n$.
- The **trace** and **determinant** of $A$ are similarity invariants: $\text{tr}(A) = \sum \lambda_i$ and $\det(A) = \prod \lambda_i$.
- For **distinct eigenvalues**, JCF is purely **diagonal**; for **repeated eigenvalues**, JCF has **Jordan blocks** with 1's on the super-diagonal.
- All three forms have the **same transfer function** and the **same characteristic polynomial** — they are mathematically equivalent representations of the same physical system.
- The **Controllability Matrix** $\mathcal{C} = [B \; AB \; A^2B \; \dots]$ takes a clean lower-triangular form in CCF.
- The **Observability Matrix** $\mathcal{O} = [C^T \; A^TC^T \; \dots]^T$ takes a clean upper-triangular form in OCF.
- The transformation $X = MZ$ (where $M$ is the modal matrix) **decouples** the system into independent first-order modes — the foundation of **modal control**.
- **Stability** is determined solely by eigenvalue locations: all in the open left-half plane $\Rightarrow$ asymptotically stable.
- For a system to be **controllable**, the controllability matrix must have **full rank** $n$.
- For a system to be **observable**, the observability matrix must have **full rank** $n$.
- The **transformation matrix** between CCF and OCF is given by: $P = \mathcal{C} \cdot \mathcal{O}^{-1}$ (in the controllable form), or equivalently, $P = A_c^{n-1} + a_1 A_c^{n-2} + \dots + a_{n-1} I$.
- **MATLAB check:** Use `tf2ss(G, 'canonical')` or simply `[A,B,C,D] = tf2ss(num, den)` — MATLAB returns the **CCF by default** for SISO systems.
- **Direct-feedthrough term:** $D = b_0$ in all three canonical forms (it's the high-frequency gain).
- **Reversed order in $C_c$:** The matrix $C_c$ is written in the order $(b_n, b_{n-1}, \dots, b_1)$, matching the reversed ordering of the $A_c$ columns $(-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \dots, -a_1)$.
- **Eigenvector computation:** For a 2×2 system, $v_i = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ \lambda_i - a_{11} \end{bmatrix}$ is a quick choice if $a_{11}$ is the $(1,1)$ entry of $A$.
- **Common exam trap:** A first-order system ($n=1$) has CCF: $A_c = [-a_1]$, $B_c = [1]$, $C_c = [b_1 - a_1 b_0]$ — always a single number/scalar, not a matrix.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Variable Analysis of Continuous LTI Systems and Solution of State Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT604 - MODERN CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** State Variable Analysis of Continuous LTI Systems and Solution of State Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: State Variable Analysis

**State** of a dynamic system is the minimal set of variables (called **state variables**) such that the knowledge of these variables at any initial time $t_0$, together with the knowledge of the input for $t \geq t_0$, completely determines the future behaviour of the system for all $t \geq t_0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBICT604 - Module 3):**
> The **state-space representation** of a continuous-time LTI system is a mathematical model expressed as a set of first-order differential equations in matrix form:
> $$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$$
> $$y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$$
> where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the **system matrix**, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ is the **input matrix**, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ is the **output matrix**, and $D \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ is the **feed-through (direct transmission) matrix**.

**State Vector:** $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the column vector containing all $n$ state variables.

**State Space:** The $n$-dimensional space whose coordinate axes are the $n$ state variables. A point in this space is called the **state point**, and its trajectory is called the **state trajectory**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Memory + Engine" Picture**
> Imagine driving a car up a hill. To predict the car's future position, you don't just need to know where it is *right now* — you also need to know its **velocity** and the **throttle input** (acceleration). The state variables (position, velocity) act as the system's *internal memory* — they carry the past forward. The differential equation is the *engine* that updates these memories every instant. The **state-space model** is simply a compact, structured way of writing these engine rules so that a computer (or a control engineer) can predict the future from the present plus the input.

**Real-World Engineering Context:** In modern control engineering, state-space methods underpin:
- **Aerospace:** Aircraft autopilot design (full 6-DOF state: position + velocity + orientation + angular velocity).
- **Robotics:** Robotic arm trajectory tracking (joint angles and angular velocities).
- **Power Systems:** Multi-machine grid stability analysis.
- **Process Control:** MIMO (multi-input multi-output) chemical plant control.

**Why do we need state variables instead of transfer functions?**

| Aspect | Transfer Function (Classical) | State-Space (Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| Order | Limited to SISO easily | Handles **MIMO** naturally |
| Initial Conditions | Hard to incorporate | Built-in via $x(0)$ |
| Internal Variables | Hidden (black box) | Explicit state variables |
| Design Methods | Root locus, Bode | Pole placement, LQR, observers |

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **LTI:** Linear Time-Invariant — the $A, B, C, D$ matrices are constant (time-invariant) and the system obeys superposition (linear).
- **Order of the system ($n$):** Number of state variables, equal to the order of the governing differential equation.
- **Phase variables:** A specific choice of state variables where the first is the output, the second is its first derivative, and so on.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For an $n^{th}$ order LTI system described by a single ODE, there exist an **infinite number of valid state-space representations** (related by similarity transformations $T$), but **all of them have the same transfer function** and the **same characteristic polynomial** $\det(sI - A) = $ denominator of $G(s)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Standard Form of the State-Space Model

The **complete** state-space description has two parts:

**State Equation (updates the state):**

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)
$$

**Output Equation (computes the output):**

$$
y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)
$$

For a SISO system: $u$ is scalar, $y$ is scalar, $x$ is $n \times 1$.

### 2.2 Deriving State-Space from an $n^{th}$-Order ODE

Consider a general $n^{th}$-order linear ODE with constant coefficients:

$$
\frac{d^n y}{dt^n} + a_1 \frac{d^{n-1} y}{dt^{n-1}} + \cdots + a_{n-1} \frac{dy}{dt} + a_n y = b_0 \frac{d^n u}{dt^n} + b_1 \frac{d^{n-1} u}{dt^{n-1}} + \cdots + b_{n-1} \frac{du}{dt} + b_n u
$$

**Step-by-step conversion logic:**

1. **Identify $n$** (order of the differential equation) — this is the number of state variables.
2. **Choose phase variables:**
   - $x_1 = y$ (output)
   - $x_2 = \dot{y}$ (first derivative of output)
   - $x_3 = \ddot{y}$
   - $\vdots$
   - $x_n = y^{(n-1)}$
3. **Express derivatives as recursive relations** using the chosen state variables.
4. **Solve the ODE algebraically** for the highest derivative $\frac{d^n y}{dt^n}$ in terms of the lower derivatives and the input.
5. **Write the $n$ first-order equations** and arrange into matrix form.

### 2.3 Canonical Forms — The Four Pillars

#### (A) Controllable Canonical Form (CCF) — Phase Variable Form

For a SISO system with transfer function:

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{b_0 s^n + b_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + b_{n-1} s + b_n}{s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_{n-1} s + a_n}
$$

(assuming $b_0 = 0$ for strictly proper, with appropriate modifications otherwise)

The state equations in **Controllable Canonical Form** are:

$$
A_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_n & -a_{n-1} & -a_{n-2} & \cdots & -a_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
C_c = \begin{bmatrix} (b_n - a_n b_0) & (b_{n-1} - a_{n-1} b_0) & \cdots & (b_1 - a_1 b_0) \end{bmatrix}, \quad
D_c = b_0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory aid for CCF:** The bottom row of $A_c$ contains **negatives of the denominator coefficients** (in reverse order). The bottom row of $C_c$ contains **numerator coefficients minus $b_0$ times denominator coefficients**. $B_c$ has a single $1$ at the bottom.

#### (B) Observable Canonical Form (OCF)

This is the **dual** of the CCF. Swap the roles of $C \leftrightarrow B^T$ and transpose $A$:

$$
A_o = A_c^T, \quad B_o = C_c^T, \quad C_o = B_c^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_o = D_c
$$

Explicitly:

$$
A_o = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & -a_n \\ 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & -a_{n-1} \\ 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 & -a_{n-2} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & -a_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B_o = \begin{bmatrix} b_n - a_n b_0 \\ b_{n-1} - a_{n-1} b_0 \\ \vdots \\ b_1 - a_1 b_0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### (C) Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)

If the system has $n$ **distinct real eigenvalues** $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$, then $A$ can be put in **diagonal form** via similarity transformation $x = Mz$ where $M$ is the modal matrix (columns are eigenvectors):

$$
A_d = M^{-1} A M = \text{diag}\{\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n\}
$$

The state equations are **decoupled** (each $\dot{z}_i$ depends only on $z_i$ and $u$):

$$
\dot{z}_i = \lambda_i z_i + \beta_i u, \quad y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i z_i + D u
$$

where $\alpha_i, \beta_i$ are the entries of $C M$ and $M^{-1} B$ respectively.

#### (D) Jordan Canonical Form (JCF)

If $A$ has **repeated eigenvalues** (e.g., $\lambda_1$ with multiplicity $m$), the matrix can be diagonalized only if there are $m$ linearly independent eigenvectors. If not, the canonical form contains **Jordan blocks** $J_k(\lambda)$ of size $k$:

$$
J_k(\lambda) = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & \lambda \end{bmatrix}_{k \times k}
$$

The super-diagonal contains 1's grouped at positions corresponding to the algebraic multiplicity of the repeated eigenvalue.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Board Examiner's "Must-Know" Formula Table for Module 3:**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State Equation (continuous-time LTI) | $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ | System dynamics in matrix form |
| 2 | Output Equation | $y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$ | Output depends linearly on state & input |
| 3 | Transfer Function from State-Space | $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1} B + D$ | External description (SISO/MIMO) |
| 4 | Characteristic Polynomial | $\Delta(s) = \det(sI - A)$ | Equals denominator of $G(s)$ for SISO |
| 5 | Eigenvalues of $A$ | Roots of $\det(sI - A) = 0$ | Natural modes of the system |
| 6 | Similarity Transformation | $\bar{A} = T^{-1} A T$, $\bar{B} = T^{-1} B$, $\bar{C} = CT$ | Change of basis (infinite representations) |
| 7 | **State Transition Matrix (STM)** | $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$ | Maps $x(t_0)$ to $x(t)$ for zero input |
| 8 | STM via Laplace Transform | $\Phi(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$ | Standard computational method |
| 9 | **Time-Domain Solution (zero input)** | $x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0)$ | For $u(t) = 0$ |
| 10 | **Time-Domain Solution (general)** | $x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0) + \int_0^t \Phi(t - \tau) B u(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Convolution form (zero-state + zero-input) |
| 11 | Laplace-Domain Solution | $X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} x(0) + (sI - A)^{-1} B U(s)$ | Direct from Laplace transforms |
| 12 | Initial State Identity | $\Phi(0) = I$ | Identity at $t = 0$ |
| 13 | Composition Property | $\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \Phi(t_1) \Phi(t_2)$ | Semigroup property |
| 14 | Inverse Property | $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$ | Invertibility for all $t$ |
| 15 | Differentiation Property | $\dot{\Phi}(t) = A \Phi(t) = \Phi(t) A$ | Derivative commutes with $A$ |
| 16 | Output Time Response | $y(t) = C \Phi(t) x(0) + \int_0^t C \Phi(t - \tau) B u(\tau) d\tau + D u(t)$ | Complete forced response |
| 17 | Cayley-Hamilton | $f(A) = 0$ where $f(s) = \det(sI - A)$ | Used to compute $e^{At}$ |
| 18 | DCF Decomposition | $A_d = M^{-1} A M$ with $M$ = modal matrix | Diagonalization with distinct eigenvalues |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on absolute values:** In the above table, all bars (e.g., $\bar{A}$, $\bar{B}$, $\bar{C}$) are used to denote *transformed* (new-basis) matrices — **not** absolute value. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ only for true absolute value in your own working.

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of State-Space Solutions:**
> - **Control Design:** Pole placement and LQR require solving the *Lyapunov* equation $A^T P + P A + Q = 0$, which uses the STM.
> - **Observer Design (Luenberger, Kalman):** Observers use the STM to predict state evolution.
> - **Stability Analysis:** As $t \to \infty$, $\Phi(t) \to 0$ if and only if all eigenvalues of $A$ have negative real parts (asymptotic stability).
> - **Digital Simulation:** The STM is the cornerstone of numerical integration schemes for control system simulation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: $n^{th}$-Order ODE $\rightarrow$ State-Space (CCF)

**Starting ODE:**

$$
\ddot{y} + 6\ddot{y} + 11\dot{y} + 6y = \dot{u} + 2u
$$

(For KTU brevity, we'll use a 3rd-order example. The pattern generalizes.)

**Step 1 — Identify $n$ and divide by leading coefficient:**

Here $n = 3$. Divide through by the coefficient of the highest derivative (which is 1 already):

$$
y^{(3)} + 6 y^{(2)} + 11 y^{(1)} + 6 y = u^{(1)} + 2u
$$

**Step 2 — Match with standard form coefficients:**

- $a_1 = 6$, $a_2 = 11$, $a_3 = 6$
- $b_0 = 0$, $b_1 = 0$, $b_2 = 1$, $b_3 = 2$

(The numerator is $0\cdot s^3 + 0\cdot s^2 + 1\cdot s + 2 = s + 2$.)

**Step 3 — Define phase variables:**

$$
x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad x_3 = \ddot{y}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the recursive relations:**

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \quad \dot{x}_2 = x_3
$$

For $\dot{x}_3$, solve the original ODE for $y^{(3)}$:

$$
\dot{x}_3 = y^{(3)} = -6 y^{(2)} - 11 y^{(1)} - 6 y + \dot{u} + 2u
$$

Now we have an unwanted $\dot{u}$ term. We eliminate it by **introducing an auxiliary state** $x_4 = u$ (since $b_0 = 0$, we do not need this; for the strictly-proper case $b_0 = 0$):

Wait — to keep this clean for KTU standard questions (where the numerator degree is strictly less than denominator), we can take a simpler 2nd-order example.

**Simplified 2nd-Order Example (Strictly Proper):**

$$
\ddot{y} + 5\dot{y} + 6y = 2u
$$

- $n = 2$
- $a_1 = 5$, $a_2 = 6$
- Numerator is just $2$, so $b_0 = 0$, $b_1 = 0$, $b_2 = 2$

**Phase variables:** $x_1 = y$, $x_2 = \dot{y}$

**Recursive equations:**

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = \ddot{y} = -5\dot{y} - 6y + 2u = -6x_1 - 5x_2 + 2u
$$

**Output equation:**

$$
y = x_1
$$

**Matrix form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} u
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Verify** by computing $C(sI-A)^{-1}B$:

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 6 & s+5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 5s + 6} \begin{bmatrix} s+5 & 1 \\ -6 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
C(sI-A)^{-1}B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{s^2+5s+6}\begin{bmatrix} s+5 & 1 \\ -6 & s \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{s^2+5s+6} \begin{bmatrix} s+5 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{s^2+5s+6} = \frac{2}{(s+2)(s+3)} \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Matches the transfer function** derived from the ODE: $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s^2 + 5s + 6}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The State Transition Matrix via Laplace Transform

**Starting point:** The state equation $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$.

**Step 1 — Take the Laplace transform of both sides:**

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = \mathcal{L}\{Ax(t)\} + \mathcal{L}\{Bu(t)\}
$$

Using the derivative rule $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}\} = sX(s) - x(0)$:

$$
sX(s) - x(0) = A X(s) + B U(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Collect $X(s)$ terms on the left:**

$$
sX(s) - A X(s) = x(0) + B U(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $X(s)$ using the matrix identity $sX - AX = (sI - A)X$:**

$$
(sI - A) X(s) = x(0) + B U(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Pre-multiply by $(sI - A)^{-1}$ (assuming $sI - A$ is invertible, i.e., $s$ is not an eigenvalue of $A$):**

$$
X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} x(0) + (sI - A)^{-1} B U(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Take the inverse Laplace transform:**

$$
x(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1}\} x(0) + \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1} B U(s)\}
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the State Transition Matrix:**

$$
\Phi(t) \triangleq e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the convolution theorem** to the second term (product in $s$-domain = convolution in $t$-domain):

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1} B U(s)\} = \int_0^t \Phi(t - \tau) B u(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Final result — The General State Equation Solution:**

$$
\boxed{\,x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0) + \int_0^t \Phi(t - \tau) B u(\tau) \, d\tau\,}
$$

where $\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two terms have a clear physical meaning:
> - $\Phi(t) x(0)$ → **zero-input response** (natural response / homogeneous solution).
> - $\int_0^t \Phi(t - \tau) B u(\tau) \, d\tau$ → **zero-state response** (forced response / particular solution).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: State Transition Matrix via Cayley-Hamilton Theorem

**The Cayley-Hamilton Theorem states:** Every square matrix $A$ satisfies its own characteristic equation.

If $\Delta(s) = \det(sI - A) = s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_{n-1} s + a_n I$, then:

$$
A^n + a_1 A^{n-1} + \cdots + a_{n-1} A + a_n I = 0
$$

**Step 1 — Write the matrix exponential as a power series:**

$$
e^{At} = I + At + \frac{A^2 t^2}{2!} + \frac{A^3 t^3}{3!} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At)^k}{k!}
$$

**Step 2 — Use Cayley-Hamilton to reduce $A^n$ to a linear combination of lower powers:**

For a 3rd-order system:

$$
A^3 = -a_1 A^2 - a_2 A - a_3 I
$$

So $e^{At}$ can be expressed using only $I, A, A^2$ (and analogously for higher orders):

$$
e^{At} = \alpha_0(t) I + \alpha_1(t) A + \alpha_2(t) A^2
$$

**Step 3 — Use the Cayley-Hamilton result applied to the scalar $\lambda_i$ (an eigenvalue):**

For each eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ of $A$:

$$
e^{\lambda_i t} = \alpha_0(t) + \alpha_1(t) \lambda_i + \alpha_2(t) \lambda_i^2
$$

This gives $n$ scalar equations in $n$ unknowns $\alpha_0, \alpha_1, \alpha_2$, which can be solved by matrix inversion.

**Step 4 — For distinct eigenvalues** $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \lambda_1 & \lambda_1^2 \\ 1 & \lambda_2 & \lambda_2^2 \\ 1 & \lambda_3 & \lambda_3^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \alpha_0(t) \\ \alpha_1(t) \\ \alpha_2(t) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} e^{\lambda_1 t} \\ e^{\lambda_2 t} \\ e^{\lambda_3 t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solve for $\alpha_i(t)$ by inversion, then substitute back into $\Phi(t) = \alpha_0(t) I + \alpha_1(t) A + \alpha_2(t) A^2$.

**Step 5 — For repeated eigenvalues** (e.g., $\lambda_1$ of multiplicity 2), differentiate the corresponding Cayley-Hamilton equation with respect to $\lambda$:

$$
t e^{\lambda_1 t} = \alpha_1(t) + 2\alpha_2(t) \lambda_1
$$

This gives the extra equation needed when one eigenvalue is repeated.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: State Transition Matrix Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Properties of $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$ that KTU expects you to state AND prove:**

**Property 1: Initial Condition Identity**

$$
\Phi(0) = e^{A \cdot 0} = e^0 = I
$$

**Property 2: Composition (Semigroup) Property**

$$
\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = e^{A(t_1 + t_2)} = e^{At_1} \cdot e^{At_2} = \Phi(t_1) \Phi(t_2)
$$

This follows from the algebraic law of exponents for commuting scalars (since $A$ commutes with itself and with $t_1 I$, $t_2 I$).

**Property 3: Inverse Property**

$$
\Phi^{-1}(t) = e^{-At} = \Phi(-t)
$$

*Proof:* $\Phi(t) \cdot \Phi(-t) = e^{At} e^{-At} = e^{A(t-t)} = e^0 = I$. Therefore $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$.

**Property 4: Differentiation Property**

$$
\dot{\Phi}(t) = A e^{At} = e^{At} A = A \Phi(t) = \Phi(t) A
$$

*Proof:* Differentiate the power series term-by-term:

$$
\dot{\Phi}(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(At)^k}{k!} = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{k A^k t^{k-1}}{k!} = A \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(At)^k}{k!} = A \Phi(t)
$$

**Property 5: Power Property**

$$
[\Phi(t)]^k = \Phi(kt) = e^{Akt}
$$

*Proof:* By induction using Property 2: $[\Phi(t)]^2 = \Phi(t)\Phi(t) = \Phi(2t)$, and so on.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Full State-Space Analysis

**Problem:** Consider the system $\ddot{y} + 3\dot{y} + 2y = u$.

**(a) Derive the state-space representation in CCF.**

**Solution:**

- $a_1 = 3$, $a_2 = 2$, $n = 2$, $b_0 = 0$, $b_1 = 0$, $b_2 = 1$.
- Phase variables: $x_1 = y$, $x_2 = \dot{y}$.

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -2x_1 - 3x_2 + u
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(b) Find the State Transition Matrix using the Laplace method.**

**Solution:**

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\det(sI - A) = s(s+3) - (-1)(2) = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)
$$

Using the adjugate method:

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

Partial-fraction each entry:

$$
\frac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{1}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}
$$

$$
\frac{-2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-2}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}
$$

$$
\frac{s}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-1}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}
$$

Inverse Laplace:

$$
\Phi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Verify** $\Phi(0) = I$:

$$
\Phi(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 - 1 & 1 - 1 \\ -2 + 2 & -1 + 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \checkmark
$$

**(c) Find the system response $y(t)$ for $x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ and unit step input $u(t) = 1, t \geq 0$.**

**Solution:**

First, the zero-input response:

$$
x_{zi}(t) = \Phi(t) x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Next, the zero-state response. For $u(\tau) = 1$ (constant):

$$
x_{zs}(t) = \int_0^t \Phi(t - \tau) B \cdot 1 \, d\tau = \int_0^t \Phi(t - \tau) \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} d\tau
$$

The second column of $\Phi(t - \tau)$ is $\begin{bmatrix} e^{-(t-\tau)} - e^{-2(t-\tau)} \\ -e^{-(t-\tau)} + 2e^{-2(t-\tau)} \end{bmatrix}$. Integrate term by term (using $\int_0^t e^{-(t-\tau)} d\tau = 1 - e^{-t}$, and similarly for $e^{-2(t-\tau)}$):

$$
x_{zs}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} (1 - e^{-t}) - \frac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2t}) \\ -(1 - e^{-t}) + (1 - e^{-2t}) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2} - e^{-t} + \frac{1}{2}e^{-2t} \\ e^{-2t} - e^{-t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Total state: $x(t) = x_{zi}(t) + x_{zs}(t)$:

$$
x(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2} + e^{-t} - \frac{1}{2}e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Output:** $y = x_1$:

$$
\boxed{\,y(t) = \frac{1}{2} + e^{-t} - \frac{1}{2}e^{-2t}\,}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Steady-state check:** $y(\infty) = \frac{1}{2}$, which matches the DC gain of $G(s) = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)}$ at $s = 0$ (i.e., $G(0) = \frac{1}{2}$). ✓

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 State-Space Model — Block Diagram

The canonical block-diagram representation of the state-space model:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U["u(t) Input"] --> Sum1((+))
    Sum1 --> Integ1["∫ dt → x1"]
    Sum1 --> Integ2["∫ dt → x2"]
    Sum1 --> IntegDots["∫ dt → xn"]
    Integ1 --> State1["x1(t)"]
    Integ2 --> State2["x2(t)"]
    IntegDots --> StateN["xn(t)"]
    State1 --> FbA11["A11"]
    State2 --> FbA21["A21"]
    StateN --> FbAN1["An1"]
    FbA11 --> Sum1
    FbA21 --> Sum1
    FbAN1 --> Sum1
    Bmat["B Matrix (n×m)"] --> Sum1
    State1 --> CmatOut["C Matrix (p×n)"]
    State2 --> CmatOut
    StateN --> CmatOut
    CmatOut --> Yout["y(t) Output"]
    U --> Dmat["D Matrix"]
    Dmat --> Yout
```

**Block description:** Each integrator output is a state variable. The state is fed back through the $A$ matrix to the summer (along with the input $B u$). The output combines the state via $C$ and input via $D$.

### 4.2 Process Flow: From ODE to Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Start: nth-order ODE"]) --> Step1["Step 1: Identify n and coefficients a1..an, b0..bn"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Step 2: Choose state variables (phase vars)"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Step 3: Solve ODE for highest derivative"]
    Step3 --> Step4["Step 4: Write n first-order equations"]
    Step4 --> Step5["Step 5: Assemble into matrix form A, B, C, D"]
    Step5 --> Step6["Step 6: Verify by computing C(sI-A)^-1 B = G(s)"]
    Step6 --> Step7["Step 7: Find state transition matrix Phi(t) = L^-1{(sI-A)^-1}"]
    Step7 --> Step8["Step 8: Compute complete response: x(t) = Phi(t)x(0) + convolution integral"]
    Step8 --> End(["Output: y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)"])
```

### 4.3 State Transition Matrix Computation — Three Methods Compared

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Need["Need: Phi(t) = exp(At)"] --> M1{"Method 1: Laplace Transform"}
    Need --> M2{"Method 2: Power Series"}
    Need --> M3{"Method 3: Cayley-Hamilton"}
    M1 --> S1["Compute (sI - A)^-1"]
    S1 --> S2["Partial-fraction each entry"]
    S2 --> S3["Inverse Laplace → Phi(t)"]
    M2 --> P1["Expand exp(At) = I + At + A^2 t^2 / 2! + ..."]
    P1 --> P2["Truncate (poor for large t)"]
    M3 --> C1["Find eigenvalues of A"]
    C1 --> C2{"Distinct or Repeated?"}
    C2 -->|Distinct| C3["Solve Vandermonde system for alpha_i(t)"]
    C2 -->|Repeated| C4["Differentiate eigenvalue equation for multiplicity"]
    C3 --> C5["Build Phi(t) = sum alpha_i(t) A^i"]
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> Out["Result: Phi(t)"]
    S3 --> Out
    P2 --> Out
```

### 4.4 Eigenvalue Stability Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Evals["Eigenvalues of A"] --> Check{"Re(lambda_i) < 0 for all i?"}
    Check -->|"Yes"| Stable["Asymptotically Stable: Phi(t) → 0 as t → ∞"]
    Check -->|"No, some = 0"| Marginal["Marginally Stable (undamped oscillation)"]
    Check -->|"No, some > 0"| Unstable["Unstable: Phi(t) → ∞ as t → ∞"]
    Stable --> Eng["Engineering: System returns to rest"]
    Unstable --> Eng
```

### 4.5 State-Space Canonical Form Conversion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G["Transfer Function G(s)"] --> CCF["Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)"]
    G --> OCF["Observable Canonical Form (OCF)"]
    G --> DCF["Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)"]
    G --> JCF["Jordan Canonical Form (JCF)"]
    CCF --> Check1{"Eigenvalues distinct?"}
    Check1 -->|"Yes"| DCF2["Convert to DCF via modal matrix M"]
    Check1 -->|"No, repeated"| JCF2["Convert to JCF (with 1's on super-diagonal)"]
    OCF --> Dual["OCF is the dual of CCF"]
    CCF --> Dual
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "state" of a dynamic system. What is meant by state variables and state space?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **State** of a dynamic system is the smallest set of variables (called state variables) such that the knowledge of these variables at any time $t_0$, together with the input applied for $t \geq t_0$, completely determines the future behaviour of the system for all $t \geq t_0$.
>
> **State variables** are the minimal set of independent variables that fully describe the system's internal energy-storing status. The number of state variables equals the order of the governing differential equation.
>
> **State space** is the $n$-dimensional vector space whose axes are the $n$ state variables. The vector $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is called the state vector, and as time progresses, the tip of this vector traces a path called the **state trajectory**.
>
> *(1 mark each concept)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the state transition matrix? List any three of its properties.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **State Transition Matrix (STM)** $\Phi(t)$ is the matrix that maps the initial state $x(0)$ to the state at any later time $t$ for a zero-input LTI system:
>
> $$x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0), \quad \text{where } \Phi(t) = e^{At}$$
>
> It can be computed as $\Phi(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$.
>
> **Three key properties** (any 3 of the following 5 — KTU expects 3 here):
>
> 1. $\Phi(0) = I$ (initial identity)
> 2. $\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \Phi(t_1)\Phi(t_2)$ (composition/semigroup)
> 3. $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$ (inverse property)
> 4. $\dot{\Phi}(t) = A\Phi(t) = \Phi(t)A$ (differentiation)
> 5. $[\Phi(t)]^k = \Phi(kt)$ (power property)

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the state-space representation in **Controllable Canonical Form** for the system described by the transfer function:

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{s + 2}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}
$$

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system matrix:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

compute the state transition matrix $\Phi(t)$ using the **Laplace transform method**. Hence find the zero-input response if the initial state is $x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Deriving the state-space representation in CCF:**

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients:**

Comparing $G(s) = \dfrac{s + 2}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$ with the standard form:

$$G(s) = \frac{b_0 s^3 + b_1 s^2 + b_2 s + b_3}{s^3 + a_1 s^2 + a_2 s + a_3}$$

- Denominator: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6 \Rightarrow a_1 = 6, \; a_2 = 11, \; a_3 = 6$
- Numerator: $0\cdot s^3 + 0\cdot s^2 + 1\cdot s + 2 \Rightarrow b_0 = 0, \; b_1 = 0, \; b_2 = 1, \; b_3 = 2$
- Order $n = 3$.

**Step 2 — Write the CCF matrices directly:**

$$
A_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -a_3 & -a_2 & -a_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
C_c = \begin{bmatrix} (b_3 - a_3 b_0) & (b_2 - a_2 b_0) & (b_1 - a_1 b_0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
D_c = b_0 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — State and output equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification** (for partial credit): $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1} B$ should give the original transfer function. (Optional in exam — 1 mark bonus if done correctly.)

---

**(b) Computing $\Phi(t)$ and zero-input response:**

**Step 1 — Form $sI - A$:**

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 6 & s + 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inverse:**

$$
\det(sI - A) = s(s+5) + 6 = s^2 + 5s + 6 = (s+2)(s+3)
$$

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)} \begin{bmatrix} s+5 & 1 \\ -6 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Partial-fraction expansion of each entry:**

Entry (1,1): $\dfrac{s+5}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \dfrac{3}{s+2} - \dfrac{2}{s+3}$

Entry (1,2): $\dfrac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \dfrac{1}{s+2} - \dfrac{1}{s+3}$

Entry (2,1): $\dfrac{-6}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \dfrac{-6}{s+2} + \dfrac{6}{s+3}$

Entry (2,2): $\dfrac{s}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \dfrac{-2}{s+2} + \dfrac{3}{s+3}$

**Step 4 — Inverse Laplace transform each entry:**

$$
\Phi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 3e^{-2t} - 2e^{-3t} & e^{-2t} - e^{-3t} \\ -6e^{-2t} + 6e^{-3t} & -2e^{-2t} + 3e^{-3t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Zero-input response with $x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$:**

$$
x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} (3e^{-2t} - 2e^{-3t})\cdot 2 + (e^{-2t} - e^{-3t})\cdot(-1) \\ (-6e^{-2t} + 6e^{-3t})\cdot 2 + (-2e^{-2t} + 3e^{-3t})\cdot(-1) \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing element-by-element:

- $x_1(t) = 6e^{-2t} - 4e^{-3t} - e^{-2t} + e^{-3t} = 5e^{-2t} - 3e^{-3t}$
- $x_2(t) = -12e^{-2t} + 12e^{-3t} + 2e^{-2t} - 3e^{-3t} = -10e^{-2t} + 9e^{-3t}$

$$
\boxed{\,x(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 5e^{-2t} - 3e^{-3t} \\ -10e^{-2t} + 9e^{-3t} \end{bmatrix}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check at $t = 0$:** $x_1(0) = 5 - 3 = 2$ ✓ ; $x_2(0) = -10 + 9 = -1$ ✓.

As $t \to \infty$, $x(t) \to 0$ since both eigenvalues $(-2, -3)$ are in the left half-plane — confirming **asymptotic stability**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** For the system described by the differential equation:

$$
\frac{d^3 y}{dt^3} + 8\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 17\frac{dy}{dt} + 10y = \frac{d^2 u}{dt^2} + 4\frac{du}{dt} + 2u
$$

obtain the state-space representation in **Controllable Canonical Form**. Verify the result by computing the transfer function $G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B$.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad u(t) = 2, \; t \geq 0
$$

Find the complete state response $x(t)$ using the Laplace transform method.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) State-space in CCF:**

**Step 1 — Match coefficients:**

- $a_1 = 8, \; a_2 = 17, \; a_3 = 10$
- $b_0 = 0, \; b_1 = 1, \; b_2 = 4, \; b_3 = 2$

**Step 2 — Write the CCF matrices:**

$$
A_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -10 & -17 & -8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
C_c = \begin{bmatrix} (b_3 - a_3 b_0) & (b_2 - a_2 b_0) & (b_1 - a_1 b_0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
D_c = b_0 = 0
$$

So the state equations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -10 & -17 & -8 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification by computing $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1} B$:**

The characteristic polynomial is:

$$
\det(sI - A_c) = s^3 + 8s^2 + 17s + 10
$$

(Bottom-row cofactor expansion gives this directly; the determinant of a companion matrix is always the polynomial with reversed sign coefficients from the bottom row.)

By the resolvent formula:

$$
G(s) = C_c (sI - A_c)^{-1} B_c = \frac{1}{s^3 + 8s^2 + 17s + 10} \cdot C_c \cdot \text{adj}(sI - A_c) \cdot B_c
$$

The product $C_c \cdot \text{adj}(sI - A_c) \cdot B_c$ for a CCF system always yields the numerator polynomial directly (this is a known identity for companion-form matrices):

$$
G(s) = \frac{b_0 s^3 + b_1 s^2 + b_2 s + b_3}{s^3 + a_1 s^2 + a_2 s + a_3} = \frac{s^2 + 4s + 2}{s^3 + 8s^2 + 17s + 10}
$$

This matches the given transfer function derived from the ODE. **Verification complete.** ✓

---

**(b) Complete state response via Laplace method:**

**Step 1 — Laplace-domain state equation:**

$$
X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} x(0) + (sI - A)^{-1} B U(s)
$$

For $u(t) = 2$ (step), $U(s) = \dfrac{2}{s}$.

**Step 2 — Compute $(sI - A)$ and its inverse:**

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s+1 & -1 \\ 0 & s+2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\det(sI - A) = (s+1)(s+2)
$$

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+2 & 1 \\ 0 & s+1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the zero-input term $(sI-A)^{-1} x(0)$:**

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+2 + 1 \\ s+1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 \\ s+1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Partial-fraction:

- Entry (1,1): $\dfrac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{2}{s+1} - \dfrac{1}{s+2}$
- Entry (2,1): $\dfrac{s+1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{1}{s+2}$

**Step 4 — Compute the zero-state term $(sI-A)^{-1} B U(s)$:**

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+2 & 1 \\ 0 & s+1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s+1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiply by $U(s) = \dfrac{2}{s}$:

- Entry (1,1): $\dfrac{2}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{1}{s} - \dfrac{2}{s+1} + \dfrac{1}{s+2}$
- Entry (2,1): $\dfrac{2(s+1)}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{2}{s(s+2)} = \dfrac{1}{s} - \dfrac{1}{s+2}$

**Step 5 — Total $X(s)$:**

$$
X_1(s) = \left(\frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}\right) + \left(\frac{1}{s} - \frac{2}{s+1} + \frac{1}{s+2}\right) = \frac{1}{s}
$$

$$
X_2(s) = \frac{1}{s+2} + \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s+2} = \frac{1}{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Inverse Laplace:**

$$
\boxed{\,x(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad t \geq 0\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The state stays constant at the initial value because the system matrix $A$ has a special triangular structure and the constant input exactly cancels the natural decay. (Indeed, $\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_2 + 0\cdot 2 = -1 + 1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = -2x_2 + 2 = -2 + 2 = 0$ — verified by direct substitution into the state equation.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $A_c$'s last row:** Students often write $+a_n$ instead of $-a_n$. The last row of the CCF $A$-matrix MUST be $[-a_n \; -a_{n-1} \; \cdots \; -a_1]$ in that exact order. KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this single error.
> 2. **Reversed order in $C_c$:** $C_c$ uses $b_n, b_{n-1}, \ldots, b_1$ (not $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n$). Confusing the order is the most frequent mistake.
> 3. **Forgetting to divide by leading coefficient:** If the ODE has a non-unit leading coefficient on the highest derivative, you must divide the entire equation by it BEFORE writing $A, B, C, D$.
> 4. **In $C(sI-A)^{-1}B$ verification:** Don't compute the inverse symbolically; for the CCF, **use the resolvent identity directly** that the result is the ratio of the characteristic polynomial entries.
> 5. **In $\Phi(t)$ via Laplace:** Always check $\Phi(0) = I$ as a sanity test. KTU examiners reward the verification step (½ to 1 bonus mark).
> 6. **Convolution integral for zero-state:** Use $\int_0^t \Phi(t-\tau)Bu(\tau)d\tau$ — note the order $\Phi(t-\tau)$ first, then $B$, then $u(\tau)$ (this is the standard form for time-invariant systems).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Keep This Open During the Exam**

- [x] **State-space form:** $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $\;y = Cx + Du$ — memorize this exactly.
- [x] **CCF $A_c$** is a companion matrix with sub-diagonal of 1's, last row $[-a_n \cdots -a_1]$.
- [x] **CCF $B_c$** is a column of zeros with a single 1 at the bottom.
- [x] **CCF $C_c$** is the row $[(b_n - a_n b_0), (b_{n-1} - a_{n-1} b_0), \ldots, (b_1 - a_1 b_0)]$.
- [x] **OCF** is the dual: $A_o = A_c^T$, $B_o = C_c^T$, $C_o = B_c^T$.
- [x] **DCF** requires **distinct eigenvalues**; the transformation matrix is the modal matrix $M = [v_1 \; v_2 \; \cdots \; v_n]$.
- [x] **JCF** is used for **repeated eigenvalues**; it has 1's on the super-diagonal in blocks.
- [x] **State Transition Matrix** $\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1}\}$.
- [x] **General solution:** $x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0) + \int_0^t \Phi(t-\tau) B u(\tau) d\tau$.
- [x] **Output equation:** $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$.
- [x] **Five properties of $\Phi(t)$:** $\Phi(0) = I$, composition, inverse, differentiation, power.
- [x] **Laplace method** is the easiest for $\Phi(t)$ in 2×2 and 3×3 systems; **Cayley-Hamilton** is preferred for higher orders or when eigenvalues are simple.
- [x] **Stability test:** $A$ is asymptotically stable if and only if $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for every eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ of $A$.
- [x] **Similarity transformation** preserves the characteristic polynomial, eigenvalues, and transfer function, but NOT the state variables themselves.
- [x] **Order of system = number of state variables = dimension of $A$ matrix.**
- [x] **Direct transmission term $D$** is non-zero ONLY when the numerator and denominator of $G(s)$ have the same degree (and $D = b_0$ for a normalized SISO system).
- [x] **Common mistake to avoid:** The numerator coefficients in $C_c$ are written in **reverse** order compared to the polynomial. Use a pen-and-paper check: $G(s)$ must match the original transfer function when you compute $C(sI-A)^{-1}B$.

**Good luck for your KTU exam!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Transition Matrix and Its Properties; Computation of the State Transition Matrix Using Lyapunov's Method and Cayley-Hamilton Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT604 - MODERN CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** State Transition Matrix and Its Properties; Computation of the State Transition Matrix Using Lyapunov's Method and Cayley-Hamilton Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the State Transition Matrix (STM)

In modern state-space analysis, the **State Transition Matrix**, denoted by $\Phi(t)$, is fundamentally defined as the **matrix exponential of the system matrix $A$**. It encapsulates the entire unforced (homogeneous) dynamics of a linear time-invariant (LTI) continuous-time system described by:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t), \quad x(0) = x_0$$

The **formal mathematical definition** is:

$$\boxed{\Phi(t) \;=\; e^{A t} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^{k} t^{k}}{k!} \;=\; I + A t + \frac{A^{2} t^{2}}{2!} + \frac{A^{3} t^{3}}{3!} + \cdots}$$

The unforced (zero-input) response of the system is completely characterized by:

$$x(t) \;=\; \Phi(t)\, x(0)$$

Thus, $\Phi(t)$ is the linear operator that **maps the initial state $x(0)$ to the state at time $t$** along the natural trajectories of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The State Transition Matrix is *the single most important* concept in Module 3. Every analytical technique — controllability, observability, stability, solution of state equations — is fundamentally rooted in $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$.

## 1.2 The Output Transition Matrix

For a system with output equation $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$, the output transition is governed by a related matrix, but the **state transition matrix $\Phi(t)$** strictly refers to the $A$-matrix exponential.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Memory Crystal" of a Dynamic System

Imagine that you have a **magical crystal** that, when placed into a system at time $t = 0$, can perfectly predict where **every** internal state of the system will be at **any future time $t$**, **without** running the system forward. That crystal is the State Transition Matrix $\Phi(t)$.

- Each column of $\Phi(t)$ tells you: *"If I start with a unit pulse in state $i$ and nothing elsewhere, where will I be at time $t$?"*
- $\Phi(0) = I$ — the crystal reflects the system back to itself at $t = 0$ (no evolution has happened).
- $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$ — the crystal can run **backwards** in time just as easily as forwards.

**Real-world engineering analogy:** In structural mechanics, $\Phi(t)$ behaves like a **Green's function** for a vibrating system — it tells you how an impulse at one location propagates through the entire structure over time. In circuit theory, it is the natural-response propagator that determines how energy stored in capacitors and inductors decays or oscillates after the source is removed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Each entry $\phi_{ij}(t)$ of $\Phi(t)$ is the response of the $i^{th}$ state at time $t$ due to a unit initial condition in the $j^{th}$ state, with all other initial conditions zero. This is precisely the **impulse response** of each state channel.

## 1.4 Importance in KTU Modern Control Engineering

The state transition matrix is the cornerstone of:

| Application | Role of $\Phi(t)$ |
|---|---|
| **Zero-input response** | $x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0)$ |
| **Zero-state response** | Convolution with input: $\int_0^t \Phi(t-\tau) B u(\tau)\, d\tau$ |
| **Controllability Gramian** | $W_c = \int_0^\infty \Phi(t) B B^T \Phi^T(t)\, dt$ |
| **Observability Gramian** | $W_o = \int_0^\infty \Phi^T(t) C^T C \Phi(t)\, dt$ |
| **Lyapunov stability** | Solving $A^T P + P A = -Q$ |
| **Discrete-time systems** | $\Phi(k) = A^k$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of the State Transition Matrix

The state transition matrix $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$ satisfies a set of **algebraic and differential identities** that are absolutely essential for KTU university exams. Every property listed below is a *direct consequence* of the matrix exponential definition.

### Property 1 — Initial Condition Identity

$$\Phi(0) \;=\; e^{A \cdot 0} \;=\; I_{n \times n}$$

At $t = 0$, the system has undergone no evolution, so the transition is the identity map.

### Property 2 — Group Property (Transitivity)

$$\Phi(t_1 + t_2) \;=\; \Phi(t_1)\, \Phi(t_2) \;=\; \Phi(t_2)\, \Phi(t_1)$$

Evolution from $0 \to (t_1+t_2)$ is the same as evolving $0 \to t_1$ and then $t_1 \to t_2$. This follows from the law of exponents $e^{A t_1} \cdot e^{A t_2} = e^{A(t_1+t_2)}$.

### Property 3 — Inverse Property (Time Reversal)

$$\Phi^{-1}(t) \;=\; \Phi(-t)$$

The inverse of the transition matrix from $0$ to $t$ is the transition matrix from $0$ to $-t$. This is *symmetric* — i.e., $\Phi(t)$ is always invertible.

### Property 4 — Cascaded Transitions

$$\Phi(t_2 - t_1)\, \Phi(t_1 - t_0) \;=\; \Phi(t_2 - t_0)$$

### Property 5 — Power Property

$$\left[\Phi(t)\right]^{k} \;=\; \Phi(k t) \;=\; e^{A k t}$$

### Property 6 — Differential Property

$$\frac{d}{dt}\Phi(t) \;=\; A\, \Phi(t) \;=\; \Phi(t)\, A$$

This is the **matrix differential equation** that $\Phi(t)$ satisfies. It is the homogeneous part of the state equation itself, confirming that $x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0)$ is a valid solution.

### Property 7 — Commutativity with $A$

$$A \Phi(t) \;=\; \Phi(t) A \;=\; e^{A t} A$$

The matrix $A$ commutes with its own exponential.

### Property 8 — Sylvester Expansion (Form Used in Lyapunov's Method)

For $A$ with **distinct eigenvalues** $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$:

$$\Phi(t) \;=\; e^{A t} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} e^{\lambda_i t} \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{A - \lambda_j I}{\lambda_i - \lambda_j}$$

This is the foundation of Lyapunov's method.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the single most important formula sheet for Module 3 KTU exams. Memorize it completely.**

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | STM Definition | $\Phi(t) = e^{A t}$ | Matrix exponential of $A$ |
| 2 | Zero-Input Response | $x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0)$ | State at time $t$ from initial condition |
| 3 | Full Solution | $x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0) + \int_0^t \Phi(t-\tau) B u(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Complete state response |
| 4 | Initial Identity | $\Phi(0) = I$ | No evolution at $t=0$ |
| 5 | Group Property | $\Phi(t_1+t_2) = \Phi(t_1)\Phi(t_2)$ | Composition of evolutions |
| 6 | Inverse Property | $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$ | Time reversal |
| 7 | Power Property | $[\Phi(t)]^k = \Phi(kt)$ | Repeated evolution |
| 8 | Differential Property | $\dot{\Phi}(t) = A \Phi(t) = \Phi(t) A$ | STM satisfies its own ODE |
| 9 | Laplace Method | $\Phi(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$ | Frequency-domain computation |
| 10 | Series Method | $\Phi(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k t^k}{k!}$ | Power-series expansion |
| 11 | Lyapunov's Method (distinct $\lambda_i$) | $\Phi(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e^{\lambda_i t} \prod_{j \neq i} \frac{A - \lambda_j I}{\lambda_i - \lambda_j}$ | Sylvester's expansion |
| 12 | C-H Method | $e^{A t} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \alpha_k(t) A^k$ | Reduce matrix exponential to scalar ODEs |
| 13 | C-H Eigenvalue Equations | $e^{\lambda_i t} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \alpha_k(t) \lambda_i^k$ | Solves for $\alpha_k(t)$ |
| 14 | Characteristic Equation | $\det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda^n + a_1 \lambda^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n = 0$ | C-H theorem foundation |
| 15 | Discrete STM | $\Phi(k) = A^k$ | $x(k) = A^k x(0)$ |

## 2.3 Why These Methods Matter in Real Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility of State Transition Matrix Computation:**

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Computing $\Phi(t)$ for satellite attitude dynamics allows prediction of orientation under torque-free conditions — critical for mission planning.
- **Power Systems:** Transient stability analysis of multi-machine grids requires $e^{At}$ for the post-fault swing equations.
- **Robotics:** The forward kinematics of a robot arm over time uses the state transition matrix to propagate joint states.
- **Control System Design:** The **controllability** and **observability** Gramians require $\Phi(t)$ — without it, you cannot test these system properties in continuous time.
- **Numerical Simulation:** Most industrial control simulators (MATLAB/Simulink `expm`, `ss()`) compute $\Phi(t)$ using **Cayley-Hamilton** under the hood because direct series summation suffers from round-off errors.

## 2.4 Why Two Specific Methods (Lyapunov and Cayley-Hamilton)?

Both methods avoid:

1. **Direct series summation** — which is infinite and may not converge cleanly.
2. **Matrix inversion of $(sI - A)$** — which requires symbolic Laplace work for high-order $A$.
3. **Diagonalization** — which requires eigenvectors (cumbersome for repeated eigenvalues).

Instead, they reduce a **matrix function** to a **scalar polynomial** of degree $\le n-1$ in $A$, making computation purely algebraic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the State Transition Matrix from the State Equation

Starting from the **homogeneous state equation** (zero input):

$$\dot{x}(t) \;=\; A\, x(t), \qquad x(0) = x_0$$

This is a **first-order linear matrix ODE**. Its solution can be derived by **Laplacing both sides** or by **direct analogy with the scalar ODE** $\dot{x} = a x \Rightarrow x(t) = e^{at} x(0)$.

**Step 1 — Take the Laplace Transform:**

$$s X(s) - x(0) \;=\; A X(s)$$

**Step 2 — Group the $X(s)$ terms:**

$$(s I - A)\, X(s) \;=\; x(0)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $X(s)$:**

$$X(s) \;=\; (s I - A)^{-1}\, x(0)$$

**Step 4 — Take the inverse Laplace Transform:**

$$x(t) \;=\; \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{(s I - A)^{-1}\right\} x(0)$$

**Step 5 — Identify the State Transition Matrix:**

$$\boxed{\Phi(t) \;=\; \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{(s I - A)^{-1}\right\} \;=\; e^{A t}}$$

This derivation **rigorously establishes the equivalence** of the matrix exponential and the Laplace-inverse resolvent form.

## 3.2 Derivation of Lyapunov's Method (Sylvester's Expansion)

**Lyapunov's method** expresses $e^{A t}$ as a **linear combination of matrix projectors** $P_i$ associated with each distinct eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ of $A$.

### Step 1 — The Lagrangian Interpolation Foundation

Given a scalar function $f(z)$ and a polynomial $p(z) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (z - \lambda_i)$ of degree $n$ with **distinct roots** $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n$, the **Lagrange interpolation formula** states:

$$f(z) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} f(\lambda_i) \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{z - \lambda_j}{\lambda_i - \lambda_j}$$

This is the polynomial of degree $\le n-1$ that **agrees with $f$ at all $n$ points** $\lambda_i$.

### Step 2 — Apply to Matrix Function

Let $f(z) = e^{z t}$ and substitute $z \to A$:

$$e^{A t} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} e^{\lambda_i t} \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{A - \lambda_j I}{\lambda_i - \lambda_j}$$

This **defines Lyapunov's formula**. The right-hand side is a polynomial of degree $\le n-1$ in $A$, so it is well-defined.

### Step 3 — Why It Works (Cayley-Hamilton Connection)

By the **Cayley-Hamilton Theorem**, every matrix $A$ satisfies its own characteristic polynomial:

$$A^{n} + a_1 A^{n-1} + a_2 A^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} A + a_n I \;=\; 0$$

Thus, $A^n$ can be expressed in terms of $I, A, A^2, \ldots, A^{n-1}$. By induction, **every polynomial in $A$ of degree $\ge n$** can be reduced to a polynomial of degree $\le n-1$. Since $e^{At}$ is an infinite series in $A$, it collapses to a finite sum.

### Step 4 — Worked Example (Lyapunov's Method)

**Problem:** Compute $e^{At}$ for 

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4.1 — Find the eigenvalues:**

$$\det(\lambda I - A) \;=\; \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 2 & \lambda + 3 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \lambda(\lambda + 3) + 2 \;=\; \lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad (\lambda + 1)(\lambda + 2) \;=\; 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \lambda_1 = -1, \;\; \lambda_2 = -2$$

**Step 4.2 — Apply Lyapunov's formula (with $n=2$):**

$$e^{A t} \;=\; e^{\lambda_1 t} \cdot \frac{A - \lambda_2 I}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2} \;+\; e^{\lambda_2 t} \cdot \frac{A - \lambda_1 I}{\lambda_2 - \lambda_1}$$

$$= e^{-t} \cdot \frac{A + 2I}{-1 - (-2)} + e^{-2t} \cdot \frac{A + I}{-2 - (-1)}$$

$$= e^{-t} (A + 2I) - e^{-2t}(A + I)$$

**Step 4.3 — Compute the matrix products:**

$$A + 2I \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad A + I \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4.4 — Combine:**

$$e^{A t} \;=\; e^{-t} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} - e^{-2t} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 2 e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2 e^{-t} + 2 e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2 e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification at $t=0$:**

$$e^{A \cdot 0} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2 - 1 & 1 - 1 \\ -2 + 2 & -1 + 2 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; I \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result reduces to $I$ at $t=0$, confirming Property 1 of the state transition matrix.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Cayley-Hamilton Method

### Step 1 — Cayley-Hamilton Theorem Statement

If $p(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda^n + a_1 \lambda^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n$ is the characteristic polynomial, then:

$$p(A) \;=\; A^{n} + a_1 A^{n-1} + \cdots + a_{n-1} A + a_n I \;=\; 0$$

**In words:** A matrix is a root of its own characteristic polynomial.

### Step 2 — Reduction of Matrix Functions

By C-H, any power $A^k$ for $k \ge n$ can be expressed in terms of $\{I, A, A^2, \ldots, A^{n-1}\}$. Therefore, **any** function $f(A)$ that admits a power series can be rewritten:

$$f(A) \;=\; \alpha_0 I + \alpha_1 A + \alpha_2 A^2 + \cdots + \alpha_{n-1} A^{n-1}$$

For $f(A) = e^{At}$:

$$e^{A t} \;=\; \alpha_0(t) I + \alpha_1(t) A + \alpha_2(t) A^{2} + \cdots + \alpha_{n-1}(t) A^{n-1}$$

### Step 3 — The Eigenvalue Equations

Since the equality $f(A) = \sum \alpha_k A^k$ holds as a **matrix identity**, it must also hold when $A$ is replaced by each eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ (because both sides are polynomials in $A$, and polynomials in $A$ evaluated at eigenvalues give the same polynomial evaluated at those eigenvalues):

$$e^{\lambda_i t} \;=\; \alpha_0(t) + \alpha_1(t) \lambda_i + \alpha_2(t) \lambda_i^{2} + \cdots + \alpha_{n-1}(t) \lambda_i^{n-1}$$

for each $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.

### Step 4 — The Vandermonde System

The $n$ equations form a linear system in the $n$ unknowns $\alpha_0(t), \alpha_1(t), \ldots, \alpha_{n-1}(t)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \lambda_1 & \lambda_1^{2} & \cdots & \lambda_1^{n-1} \\ 1 & \lambda_2 & \lambda_2^{2} & \cdots & \lambda_2^{n-1} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & \lambda_n & \lambda_n^{2} & \cdots & \lambda_n^{n-1} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \alpha_0(t) \\ \alpha_1(t) \\ \vdots \\ \alpha_{n-1}(t) \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} e^{\lambda_1 t} \\ e^{\lambda_2 t} \\ \vdots \\ e^{\lambda_n t} \end{bmatrix}$$

The coefficient matrix is the **Vandermonde matrix** $V$. Since the $\lambda_i$ are distinct, $\det(V) \neq 0$, guaranteeing a unique solution.

### Step 5 — Worked Example (Cayley-Hamilton Method)

**Same $A$ as before:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \text{char. poly: } \lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = 0$$

**Step 5.1 — Form the C-H ansatz** (since $n = 2$, we need terms up to $A^1$):

$$e^{A t} \;=\; \alpha_0(t) I + \alpha_1(t) A$$

**Step 5.2 — Eigenvalue equations** (for $\lambda_1 = -1$, $\lambda_2 = -2$):

$$e^{-t} \;=\; \alpha_0(t) - \alpha_1(t)$$

$$e^{-2t} \;=\; \alpha_0(t) - 2 \alpha_1(t)$$

**Step 5.3 — Solve the system:**

Subtracting the first from the second:

$$e^{-2t} - e^{-t} \;=\; -\alpha_1(t) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \alpha_1(t) \;=\; e^{-t} - e^{-2t}$$

Substituting back:

$$\alpha_0(t) \;=\; e^{-t} + \alpha_1(t) \;=\; e^{-t} + e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \;=\; 2 e^{-t} - e^{-2t}$$

**Step 5.4 — Reconstruct $e^{At}$:**

$$e^{A t} \;=\; (2 e^{-t} - e^{-2t}) I + (e^{-t} - e^{-2t}) A$$

$$= (2 e^{-t} - e^{-2t}) \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + (e^{-t} - e^{-2t}) \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 2 e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2 e^{-t} + 2 e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2 e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}$$

**This is identical to the Lyapunov's method result**, confirming the **internal consistency** of the two approaches.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The Cayley-Hamilton method is *more general* than Lyapunov's method because it handles **repeated eigenvalues** as well. For repeated eigenvalues of multiplicity $m$, you take **derivatives** of the eigenvalue equation up to $(m-1)^{th}$ order, giving additional linear equations to solve.

## 3.4 Repeated Eigenvalue Case (Cayley-Hamilton Extension)

If $\lambda_1$ has multiplicity $m$, the **Cayley-Hamilton method** provides:

$$e^{\lambda_1 t} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \alpha_k(t) \lambda_1^{k}$$

$$\frac{d}{d\lambda}\!\left(e^{\lambda t}\right) \bigg|_{\lambda = \lambda_1} \;\Rightarrow\; t e^{\lambda_1 t} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k\, \alpha_k(t)\, \lambda_1^{k-1}$$

and so on, up to $(m-1)$ derivatives. This provides $m$ independent equations for the same $\lambda_1$, which is necessary because the Vandermonde matrix is singular when eigenvalues are equal.

### Worked Example — Repeated Eigenvalue

**Problem:** Compute $e^{At}$ for

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Eigenvalues:**

$$\det(\lambda I - A) \;=\; \lambda(\lambda + 2) + 1 \;=\; \lambda^2 + 2\lambda + 1 \;=\; (\lambda + 1)^2$$

So $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = -1$ (repeated, multiplicity 2).

**Step 2 — C-H ansatz:**

$$e^{A t} \;=\; \alpha_0(t) I + \alpha_1(t) A$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvalue equations:**

For $\lambda_1 = -1$:

$$e^{-t} \;=\; \alpha_0(t) - \alpha_1(t)$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $\lambda$ (treating $t$ as fixed) and setting $\lambda = \lambda_1$:

$$t e^{-t} \;=\; \alpha_1(t)$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$\alpha_1(t) \;=\; t e^{-t}$$

$$\alpha_0(t) \;=\; e^{-t} + \alpha_1(t) \;=\; e^{-t} + t e^{-t} \;=\; (1 + t) e^{-t}$$

**Step 5 — Reconstruct:**

$$e^{A t} \;=\; (1+t) e^{-t} I + t e^{-t} A$$

$$= (1+t) e^{-t} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + t e^{-t} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} (1+t) e^{-t} & t e^{-t} \\ -t e^{-t} & (1 - t) e^{-t} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification at $t = 0$:** $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I$ ✓

## 3.5 Why Both Methods Are Equivalent (But Not Identical)

| Aspect | Lyapunov's Method | Cayley-Hamilton Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Eigenvalue requirement** | Distinct eigenvalues only | Distinct or repeated |
| **Mathematical basis** | Lagrange interpolation | Cayley-Hamilton + polynomial reduction |
| **Computational form** | Matrix projectors | Scalar coefficients $\alpha_k(t)$ |
| **Complexity** | Simpler for distinct $\lambda_i$ | Uniform across all cases |
| **Generalization** | Requires special handling for repeats | General by differentiation |
| **Numerical stability** | Better-conditioned projectors | Vandermonde may be ill-conditioned |

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Role of the State Transition Matrix in System Analysis

```mermaid

    B3 --> C1[Zero Input Response x of t = phi of t times x of 0]

    B3 --> C3[Controllability Gramian]

    B1 --> D1[Method 1 Lyapunov Sylvester Expansion]
    B1 --> D2[Method 2 Cayley Hamilton Theorem]

    D1 --> E1[phi of t = sum over i of e to power lambda_i t times product of A minus lambda_j I over lambda_i minus lambda_j]
    D2 --> E2[phi of t = sum of alpha_k of t times A to power k from k = 0 to n minus 1]
    D3 --> E3[phi of t = inverse Laplace of sI minus A inverse]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Decision Logic for Choosing the Computation Method

```mermaid

    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is A small 2x2 or 3x3?}

    P1 --> P3[Compute matrix projectors A minus lambda_j I over lambda_i minus lambda_j]
    P3 --> P4[Sum over all eigenvalues]
```
