## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model QP, PBICT604, Module 4, May 2024]**

> List any **three** categories of project topics that are valid for the PBICT604 main project. Justify in one line each why each category qualifies as a *modern-control* project.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Inverted-pendulum / Cart stabilisation** — qualifies because the system is naturally written in state-space form $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ with $n = 4$ coupled states requiring a non-diagonal $A$, satisfying the MIMO/observer criterion of Step 2 in the selection methodology. **(1 mark)**
2. **Buck / Boost power converter control** — qualifies because the averaged switched model yields a bilinear state-space that, once linearised around the duty-cycle operating point, becomes a clean $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu + E d$ plant where $d$ is a load disturbance; the disturbance-input structure enables *optimal* (LQR) and *robust* ($H_{\infty}$) design. **(1 mark)**
3. **Quadrotor UAV attitude control** — qualifies because the 6-DOF rotational dynamics is inherently MIMO with strong cross-coupling between roll, pitch, and yaw channels, making classical SISO loop-shaping inadequate and *necessitating* state-feedback + observer design. **(1 mark)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model QP, PBICT604, Module 4, May 2024]**

> State the **two essential checks** a student must perform *before* committing to a project topic under the PBICT604 modern-control mandate.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Check 1 — Modern-Control Eligibility (1.5 marks):** Confirm that the plant has at least 2 internal states, can be modelled in state-space form, and exhibits at least one feature among MIMO coupling, partial-state measurement, or natural optimality criterion (so the work cannot be trivially solved by classical SISO methods).
- **Check 2 — Resource & Faculty-Expertise Match (1.5 marks):** Verify that the required hardware / software (e.g. MATLAB + Simulink license, DSP board, sensors, actuators) is accessible within the institute, and that at least one project guide has the domain expertise to supervise the design of the state-feedback / observer / optimal control stages.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **Internal-Choice Note (KTU Convention):** In a KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination paper, "Module 4" typically offers an internal choice between *Question A* and *Question B* of equal mark weight. Both are provided below with full sub-parts and complete model solutions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Project Selection & Justification Path**

> **(a)** [KTU University Exam – Model QP, PBICT604, Module 4, May 2024] **(7 marks)**
>
> A student is interested in a *robotic-arm position-control* project. Apply the **7-step selection methodology** to this domain. Specifically, derive the **state-space model** of a 2-DOF planar manipulator (link lengths $l_1$, $l_2$; masses $m_1$, $m_2$) driven by joint torques $\tau_1$, $\tau_2$, and demonstrate that the linearised system is **controllable**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Domain Vector:** With $I = [1, 0, 1]^{T}$ (mechanical + nonlinear), the relevant domain is **D4** (Robotic Manipulator) from the catalogue. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 2 — Modern-Control Eligibility:** The arm is naturally MIMO ($u = [\tau_1, \tau_2]^{T}$, $y = [q_1, q_2]^{T}$), and the $A$-matrix obtained after linearisation is non-diagonal due to centripetal coupling — so the modern-control qualifier holds. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — State-Space Derivation:**

The Euler–Lagrange equations of the 2-DOF planar arm (in joint space $q = [q_1, q_2]^{T}$) yield

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) \;=\; \tau$$

where

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 + m_2)\,l_1^{2} + m_2 l_2^{2} + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 & m_2 l_2^{2} + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 \\ m_2 l_2^{2} + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 & m_2 l_2^{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C(q, \dot{q}) = m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \begin{bmatrix} -\dot{q}_2 & -\dot{q}_1 - \dot{q}_2 \\ \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad G(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 + m_2)\,g l_1 \cos q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\ m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

Define $x = [q_1,\, q_2,\, \dot{q}_1,\, \dot{q}_2]^{T}$. The nonlinear state-space form is

$$\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q} \\ M(q)^{-1} \bigl( \tau - C(q, \dot{q})\,\dot{q} - G(q) \bigr) \end{bmatrix}$$

Linearising about the operating point $q^{\star} = [q_1^{\star}, q_2^{\star}]^{T}$, $\dot{q}^{\star} = 0$, $\tau^{\star} = G(q^{\star})$ (gravitational torque) and letting $\tilde{q} = q - q^{\star}$, $\tilde{\tau} = \tau - \tau^{\star}$ gives

$$\dot{\tilde{x}} = A \tilde{x} + B \tilde{\tau}$$

with

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0_{2 \times 2} & I_{2 \times 2} \\ -M^{-1} \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial q} \bigg\vert_{q^{\star}} & 0_{2 \times 2} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0_{2 \times 2} \\ M^{-1}(q^{\star}) \end{bmatrix}$$

**(3 marks)**

**Controllability Check:** Construct the controllability matrix

$$\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^{2}B & A^{3}B \end{bmatrix}$$

Using the block-triangular structure of $A$, it is straightforward to show that $\mathcal{C}$ reduces to a block form whose rank equals $\text{rank}(B) + \text{rank}(B\,M^{-1})$. For the planar arm with $M(q^{\star})$ symmetric positive-definite (and hence invertible) and $B$ full column rank (= 2), the rank condition $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = 4 = n$ is satisfied. The system is therefore **completely controllable**, and state-feedback design is admissible. **(3 marks)**

---

> **(b)** [KTU University Exam – Model QP, PBICT604, Module 4, May 2024] **(7 marks)**
>
> For the 2-DOF arm of part (a), design an **LQR state-feedback controller** with weighting matrices $Q = \text{diag}(10,\, 10,\, 1,\, 1)$ and $R = 0.1 I_2$. State the **Algebraic Riccati Equation** that must be solved and describe the steps to obtain $K$.

**Model Solution:**

The LQR cost functional is

$$J \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} \left( \tilde{x}^{T} Q \tilde{x} + \tilde{\tau}^{T} R \tilde{\tau} \right) dt$$

with $Q = Q^{T} \succeq 0$ and $R = R^{T} \succ 0$. **(1 mark)**

The continuous-time Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE) governing the optimal cost-to-go $P = P^{T} \succeq 0$ is

$$A^{T} P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^{T} P + Q \;=\; 0$$

**(2 marks)**

The **steps to obtain $K$** are:

1. Verify that the pair $(A, B)$ is stabilisable (here, completely controllable, so the condition is satisfied with margin). **(0.5 marks)**
2. Verify that the pair $(A, Q^{1/2})$ is detectable (here, the controllability of $(A, B)$ does not automatically give this, but for the linearised arm model the unobservable subspace is empty because $Q \succ 0$ on the position states and $C = [I_2,\, 0_2]$ effectively — the detection holds). **(0.5 marks)**
3. Solve the ARE numerically for $P$ using `care(A, B, Q, R)` in MATLAB (or the equivalent `solve_continuous_are` in SciPy). **(1 mark)**
4. Compute the optimal gain from

$$K \;=\; R^{-1} B^{T} P$$

**(1 mark)**

5. Verify stability by checking that all eigenvalues of $(A - BK)$ lie in the open left half-plane. **(0.5 marks)**
6. Validate by closed-loop simulation: a step in $\tilde{q}$ of $0.1$ rad should settle within $\sim 2$ s with minimal overshoot, given the chosen $Q$ and $R$. **(0.5 marks)**

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Project Implementation Path**

> **(a)** [KTU University Exam – Model QP, PBICT604, Module 4, May 2024] **(7 marks)**
>
> A student chooses the **DC-motor position-control** project (Domain D3). Derive the state-space model and show that the **state-feedback + observer** architecture can be designed by the **separation principle**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State-Space Derivation:** For an armature-controlled DC motor with field current held constant, the electrical and mechanical equations are

$$L_a \dfrac{di_a}{dt} + R_a i_a + K_b \dot{\theta} \;=\; v_a$$

$$J \ddot{\theta} + b \dot{\theta} \;=\; K_t i_a$$

Defining $x = [\theta,\, \dot{\theta},\, i_a]^{T}$, $u = v_a$, $y = \theta$, the state-space form is

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -\dfrac{b}{J} & \dfrac{K_t}{J} \\ 0 & -\dfrac{K_b}{L_a} & -\dfrac{R_a}{L_a} \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{L_a} \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 2 — Controllability and Observability Check:** Direct computation gives $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = 3$ and $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = 3$, so the system is **completely controllable and observable**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Regulator Design (Pole Placement):** Choose desired closed-loop poles, e.g. $\{-5,\, -5,\, -10\}$. The gain $K$ is computed by Ackermann or `place(A, B, P_d)` such that $\text{eig}(A - BK) = \{-5,\, -5,\, -10\}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Observer Design:** Only $\theta$ is measured (a typical encoder-only setup), so an observer is needed for $\dot{\theta}$ and $i_a$. Place observer poles faster, e.g. at $\{-30,\, -30,\, -35\}$, and compute $L$ such that $\text{eig}(A - LC) = \{-30,\, -30,\, -35\}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Separation Principle:** The combined controller–observer system is

$$\dot{\hat{x}} = A \hat{x} + B u + L (y - C \hat{x})$$

$$u = -K \hat{x}$$

Defining the estimation error $e = x - \hat{x}$, the closed-loop dynamics of the *augmented* system $\xi = [x^{T},\, e^{T}]^{T}$ decouple into

$$\dot{x} = (A - BK)\, x + BK\, e$$
$$\dot{e} = (A - LC)\, e$$

Because the two error/coordinate subsystems are **block-triangular** and decoupled in the eigenvalues, the **separation principle** asserts that the regulator poles (eigenvalues of $A - BK$) and the observer poles (eigenvalues of $A - LC$) can be designed **independently**. Hence the full controller is

$$u = -K \hat{x} = -K (x - e)$$

and the closed-loop characteristic polynomial is the product of the regulator and observer characteristic polynomials:

$$\det(sI - (A - BK)) \cdot \det(sI - (A - LC)) \;=\; 0$$

**(1 mark)**

---

> For the DC-motor project of part (a), propose a **complete project plan** in the form of: (i) a four-month Gantt-style timeline, (ii) a list of required software / hardware, and (iii) a **CO-mapping table** that aligns the project to at least three Course Outcomes of PBICT604.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mark-Deduction Errors**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = n$** before invoking state feedback — the design is *not justified* if the system is not controllable; 1–2 marks are routinely lost here.
> 2. **Using $C = I$ implicitly** when the sensor is an encoder — the encoder measures *only* $\theta$, not $\dot{\theta}$ or $i_a$; the observer design is invalid if $C$ is taken as identity.
> 3. **Not stating the separation principle explicitly** in the report — it is the *theoretical bridge* between regulator and observer design; without it, the combined design appears ad hoc.
> 4. **Mapping the project only to classical-control COs** — if the report cites "Bode plot" or "root locus" as the *primary* analysis tool, the KTU examiner will mark the modern-control linkage as missing.
> 5. **Failing to perform a unit / dimensional check** on the state-space matrices (e.g. inconsistent time units between $A$ and $B$ when a milliseconds-vs-seconds slip occurs) — this costs at least 1 mark in numerical-evaluation problems.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Suggestion on Project Topics" (Module 4, PBICT604)**

- **Core Definition:** A valid PBICT604 project must be a *modern-control* project, i.e. anchored in *state-space* (time-domain) analysis, NOT in classical transfer-function / frequency-domain methods.
- **Six Primary Project Tiers:** (1) Mechanical (pendulum, Maglev, vibration); (2) Electrical (DC motor, converter, microgrid); (3) Robotics (arm, quadrotor, AGV, swarm); (4) Process / Biomedical (CSTR, prosthetic); (5) Cyber-Physical (networked, IoT); (6) Vision-based (arm + camera).
- **7-Step Selection Algorithm:** Domain-vector → Modern-control check → Plant modelling → Controller design → Observer design → Simulation / HIL → CO-mapping. This is a *domain-agnostic* algorithm — it works for every project in the catalogue.
- **Mandatory Pre-Design Verifications:** Controllability ($\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = n$) **and** Observability ($\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = n$) **must** be confirmed before designing $K$ and $L$.
- **Ackermann's Formula (SISO case):** $K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathcal{C}^{-1} \phi_d(A)$ — useful for single-input plants; for MIMO plants use `place()` or `lqr()`.
- **LQR Workflow:** Define $J = \int (x^{T} Q x + u^{T} R u) dt$, solve the ARE $A^{T} P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^{T} P + Q = 0$, then $K = R^{-1} B^{T} P$.
- **Separation Principle:** Regulator and observer poles can be designed *independently* because the augmented closed-loop system is block-triangular. Place observer poles 3–5× faster than regulator poles.
- **Linearisation (Jacobian):** $A = \left. \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} \right\vert_{x_0, u_0}$, $B = \left. \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial u} \right\vert_{x_0, u_0}$ — required for all nonlinear plants (pendulum, arm, quadrotor).
- **Discretisation (ZOH):** $\Phi = e^{A T_s}$, $\Gamma = \int_{0}^{T_s} e^{A \tau} d\tau \, B$ — required for any digital / sampled-data implementation.
- **CO Mapping:** Every project must explicitly map to ≥ 3 of the 5 COs of PBICT604 in a one-page table inside the report.
- **Examiner Pitfall Avoidance:** Verify C/O ranks; do not assume $C = I$; explicitly state the separation principle; prefer $K$ from `place()` / `lqr()` over trial-and-error; check dimensional consistency of all matrices.
- **Industry Relevance:** Fly-by-wire aircraft, EV active suspension, drone flight controllers, prosthetic gait, and grid-forming inverters are all built on the *exact same* state-space toolset a student uses in their project — making this module's project work a direct pathway to industry-ready skills.
- **Difficulty vs Hardware Cost Trade-off:** A simulation-only project on a complex nonlinear plant (e.g. quadrotor) can be just as publishable as a hardware project on a simpler plant (e.g. DC motor) — choose by *interest and feasibility*, not by chasing the most expensive rig.
- **Deliverables Checklist:** (a) State-space model with C/O proof, (b) Designed $K$ and $L$ with eigenvalues printed, (c) Closed-loop step-response plot, (d) Robustness test (sensor noise, disturbance), (e) Final report with CO-mapping table, (f) 10-minute viva slides.
